ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn entry for pineapple tarts; mapped to the broader tart food product class as the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_description: Biscuits, cookies and similar small baked products
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code specific to pineapple tarts; A0FKP (biscuits, cookies and similar small baked products) is the closest defensible category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for pineapple tarts; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of pineapple tarts is one to two pieces, each weighing approximately 15–30 g, commonly consumed as a festive snack during Chinese New Year, Hari Raya, or Deepavali celebrations.

nutrient_source.title: Energy and Nutrient Composition of Common Foods — Festive Snacks and Cookies
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values for pineapple tarts are adapted from the Health Promotion Board Singapore food composition data for festive cookies and pastries. Values for saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, and water are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for shortbread cookies (FDC ID 18158) and butter pastry composites, as HPB does not publish full fatty acid profiles for this item. All adaptations are documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for pineapple tarts in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 65 is estimated by analogy to shortbread cookies and butter biscuits (GI ~55–70, medium range) combined with the high refined sugar content of the pineapple jam filling. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (65 × 15.75) / 100 ≈ 10.2 for a 30 g serving (15.75 g carbohydrate). This is a conservative medium-range estimate consistent with the food's composition.

hypertension.key_factor: Moderate sodium content (120 mg/100 g) and high saturated fat from butter pastry
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit pineapple tart consumption to one or two pieces per occasion, as the combined sodium load and saturated fat content may contribute to elevated blood pressure and cardiovascular strain when consumed in large quantities.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High refined sugar content (28.5 g/100 g), medium-to-high glycemic index (~65), and high energy density (420 kcal/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Pineapple tarts are not recommended for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to their high refined sugar content and medium-to-high glycemic index; if consumed during festive occasions, strict portion control of one piece (approximately 30 g) is advised and blood glucose monitoring is recommended.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat content (~13.5 g/100 g from butter) and high energy density contributing to dyslipidaemia risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high saturated fat content from butter in pineapple tarts poses a significant concern for individuals with cardiovascular disease; consumption should be strictly limited to small portions during festive occasions, and individuals on lipid-lowering therapy should consult their clinician before including this food in their diet.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pineapple contains bromelain with anti-inflammatory properties, but high refined sugar and saturated fat may promote systemic inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: While the pineapple filling provides trace bromelain with potential anti-inflammatory benefit, the high refined sugar and saturated fat content of pineapple tarts may exacerbate systemic inflammation in individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; portion sizes should be kept to one piece per occasion.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pineapple Tarts (Tart Nanas in Malay; 凤梨酥 in Chinese; Nastar in Indonesian) are a quintessential festive cookie deeply embedded in the culinary traditions of Malaysia, Singapore, and Indonesia. They are most prominently associated with Chinese New Year, Hari Raya Aidilfitri, and Deepavali celebrations, where they are gifted in decorative tins and served to guests as a symbol of hospitality and prosperity. The cookie consists of two components: a rich, crumbly, melt-in-the-mouth buttery shortcrust pastry base made from wheat flour, butter, egg yolk, and vanilla, and a thick, slow-cooked pineapple jam filling prepared from fresh pineapples, sugar, and warm spices such as cinnamon and cloves. The jam is reduced until it achieves a deep golden-amber colour and a concentrated sweet-tart flavour. Pineapple tarts come in several forms: open-faced tarts with a pastry shell and jam topping, rolled logs with jam enclosed in pastry, and golf-ball-shaped 'nastars' where the jam is fully encased. Nutritionally, pineapple tarts are energy-dense at approximately 420 kcal per 100 g, with significant fat (22.5 g, predominantly saturated from butter at ~13.5 g/100 g) and high sugar (28.5 g/100 g from the jam). The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 65 (medium range), making them a concern for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular disease. Sodium content is moderate at 120 mg/100 g. A standard serving is one to two pieces (approximately 30 g), providing around 126 kcal and 15.75 g of carbohydrate. The food is vegetarian and halal-suitable when prepared with halal-certified butter and ingredients.
common_user_queries: How many calories in pineapple tarts?
Are pineapple tarts suitable for diabetics?
How much sugar in pineapple tarts?
meal_context: festive snack during Chinese New Year

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values are adapted from Health Promotion Board Singapore data for festive cookies and pastries, which is an approved primary regional source. Saturated fat, fatty acid fractions, cholesterol, and water content are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for shortbread cookies (FDC ID 18158) and butter pastry composites, as HPB does not publish full fatty acid profiles for this specific item. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database values for butter biscuits and shortbread, as no direct GI measurement for pineapple tarts exists in the approved GI database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (13.5 g/100 g), monounsaturated fat (6.2 g/100 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.1 g/100 g), trans_fat_g (0.3 g/100 g), cholesterol_mg (85 mg/100 g), and water_g (12.0 g/100 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy shortbread cookies (FDC ID 18158) and butter pastry composites, as the Health Promotion Board Singapore does not publish full fatty acid or moisture profiles for pineapple tarts. These values are documented as adapted estimates. 2. Vitamin A is reported as 150 mcg RAE (converted from the input value of 180 mcg retinol equivalents using standard RAE conversion factors). 3. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database values for shortbread cookies and butter biscuits (GI range 55–70); no direct GI measurement for pineapple tarts exists in the approved GI database. Glycemic load per 30 g serving = (65 × 15.75) / 100 ≈ 10.2. 4. The input record listed 'low_sodium' as a dietary tag; this has been removed as 120 mg sodium per 100 g does not meet the standard threshold for a low-sodium claim (typically ≤40 mg/100 g per HPB/FSS guidelines). 5. All core macros (protein_g, fat_g, carbohydrate_g) are non-zero and nutritionally consistent with a butter-pastry and pineapple-jam cookie product. No zero-macro justification is required. 6. No -1 sentinel values remain in the per_100g block; all fields have been populated with either sourced or estimated numeric values. 7. Q8 fix (iteration 3): aliases_by_language self-check performed — all string values across all language keys verified to appear verbatim in the flat aliases array. Confirmed: 'Pineapple Tarts', 'Pineapple Cookies', 'Pineapple Jam Tarts', 'Festive Pineapple Tarts' (english); '凤梨酥', '鳳梨酥' (chinese); 'Tart Nanas', 'Kuih Tart Nanas' (malay); 'அன்னாசி டார்ட்' (tamil); 'अनानास टार्ट' (hindi); 'Nastar', 'Nastar Cookies' (indonesian); 'Tart Nanas', 'Nastar' (romanized) — all present verbatim in flat aliases array.

Iteration 2: Updated ontology match types from 'broad' to 'closest_parent'; verified all hard gates.

Removed erroneous 'அனानास टार्ट' mixed-script entry from previous draft; replaced with correct 'अनानास टार्ट' (Hindi Devanagari) consistently across both aliases and aliases_by_language. Verified romanized entries 'Tart Nanas' and 'Nastar' are present in flat aliases. Q8 gate now passes.
